threats.title: Duplicate Holder Entries via Zero-Value Transfers Enables Reward Drain and Distribution DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: Any address (even non-approved, non-holder) can call transfer(approvedAddress, 0) repeatedly. In _beforeTokenTransfer, balanceOf(to) is checked BEFORE the transfer executes. Since to has 0 balance, exists=false and to is pushed to holders every time. The _afterTokenTransfer only tries to remove `from` (the sender), not `to`. Since the sender was never in holders, the removal is a no-op. This creates unbounded duplicate entries for any approved address. When that address later receives real tokens and distribution occurs, entitlement = erc20EntitlementPerUnit * balanceOf(recipient) is computed for EACH duplicate entry, draining the reward pool or causing reverts that permanently lock the distribution.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code-level logic bug in _beforeTokenTransfer that allows any external caller to corrupt the holders array without any special privileges. The attack requires only on-chain calls and can drain all distributable ERC20 rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: balanceOf(to) checked pre-transfer; holders.push(to) when 0; no check that amount > 0; _afterTokenTransfer only removes `from`, not `to`

preconditions: At least one address is in HolderAllowlist
LockedForDistribution is false
Attacker has any EOA (no balance or approval needed)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: transfer(approvedAddr, 0) pushes to to holders array without any amount validation; _afterTokenTransfer removes from (sender) not to (receiver)
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any external address can execute this attack

operational_questions: Can ERC20 transfer() with amount=0 pass all OZ internal checks?
Does _afterTokenTransfer ever clean up the duplicate `to` entries?
What is the maximum reward multiplier achievable before distribution reverts?
validation_requirements: Call transfer(approved_addr, 0) N times from non-holder address, verify holders array contains N duplicate entries
Transfer real tokens to the duplicated address, trigger distribution, verify N*entitlement is paid or reverts
Verify OZ ERC20 0.8.12 allows 0-amount transfers

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker identifies an approved holder address (or colludes with one) with 0 balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: From any EOA, call transfer(targetApprovedAddr, 0) N times, creating N entries in holders array
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Target address receives real tokens (via mint or transfer from another holder)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Wait for distribution - target receives N * entitlement, draining the reward pool. If N*entitlement > balance, distribution reverts permanently.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Minimal - single EOA can inflate any approved address's holder entries

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete reward pool drain or permanent distribution lock
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: If distribution reverts, LockedForDistribution stays true, freezing ALL transfers permanently
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery mechanism exists; contract is permanently bricked if distribution cannot complete
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All token transfers, all distributions, all mints and burns

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single attacker, repeated 0-value transfer calls

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Many 0-value transfer events to the same address from the same sender
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: holders array length >> number of unique token holders

threats.title: address(0) Accumulation in Holders Array via Burns Causes Unbounded Distribution DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: Every burn operation calls _beforeTokenTransfer with to=address(0). Since balanceOf(address(0)) is always 0, the check `this.balanceOf(to) != 0` is always false, causing `holders.push(address(0))` on every burn. The _afterTokenTransfer only checks and removes `from` (the burner). address(0) entries accumulate permanently. After sufficient burns, the holders array grows so large that distributeToAllHolders() exceeds the block gas limit, permanently preventing full distributions. Even partial distribute() calls waste gas iterating over address(0) entries.
threats.investigation_rationale: Burns are a normal user operation (inherited from ERC20Burnable). Each burn adds a phantom holder entry. The documentation explicitly flags OOG DoS during distribution as a risk area.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bool exists = (this.balanceOf(to) != 0); if (!exists) { holders.push(to); } — to=address(0) during burn always has balanceOf==0, unconditionally pushed

preconditions: At least one approved holder can burn tokens (public burn from ERC20Burnable)
LockedForDistribution is false
_isPastMinDistributionPeriod returns false (or burnAndDistribute completes first)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Every burn call pushes address(0) to holders; no mechanism to remove address(0) entries
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any token holder can burn their tokens
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time burns are permitted (not during distribution, not past min period)

operational_questions: How many burns are needed to make distributeToAllHolders() exceed block gas limit?
Can partial distribute() calls still complete despite phantom entries?
Is there any path to clean up address(0) entries from holders?
validation_requirements: Execute N burns and verify holders array length increases by N
Measure gas cost of distribute() with increasing address(0) entries
Determine block gas limit on target chain (Althea-L1) and calculate max safe holders length

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker obtains tokens (via owner mint or purchase from approved holder)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Burn small amounts repeatedly (e.g., burn(1) many times) to inflate holders array with address(0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Once holders array is sufficiently large, distributeToAllHolders() exceeds block gas limit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Partial distribute() calls still work but waste gas on phantom entries; if attacker continues burning, eventually even partial batches become prohibitively expensive
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single token holder can execute; cost is the burned tokens

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All mints and burns blocked once MinDistributionPeriod passes without distribution
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain recovery mechanism; contract would need to be abandoned and redeployed
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: distribute, distributeToAllHolders, mint, burn, all transfers (blocked during stuck distribution)

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Minimal - any approved holder with any balance
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single attacker, repeated burn calls

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Many small burn events from the same address; holders array length >> actual holder count
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Increasing gas costs for distribute() over time

threats.title: Integer Division Precision Loss Permanently Locks Revenue for Low-Decimal Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The entitlement calculation in _beginDistribution uses `balance / supply` (floor division) to compute per-unit entitlement. For 6-decimal tokens (USDC, USDT) with an 18-decimal LiquidInfrastructureERC20 supply, the entitlement truncates to 0 for any realistic balance. For example, 1000 USDC (1e9 units) / 1000 tokens (1e21 units) = 0. All revenue in that token is permanently undistributable. The correct formula should be `(balance * holderBalance) / supply` to preserve precision.
threats.investigation_rationale: The project documentation explicitly states USDC and USDT as target distributable tokens. Both have 6 decimals. With standard 18-decimal ERC20 supply, the math demonstrably fails. No recovery mechanism exists for trapped dust.
threats.config_evidence: Documentation states 'External Integrations: Vetted ERC20 stablecoins (e.g., USDC, DAI, USDT)'. USDC/USDT have 6 decimals.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 entitlement = balance / supply; — division before multiplication causes truncation to 0 when balance < supply

preconditions: A distributableERC20 has fewer decimals than the LiquidInfrastructureERC20 (18)
totalSupply of LiquidInfrastructureERC20 exceeds the balance of the low-decimal token
At least one holder exists

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: balance / supply uses floor division; for 6-decimal USDC vs 18-decimal supply, result is always 0 for realistic amounts
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - math is inherently wrong for low-decimal tokens regardless of admin actions
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every distribution cycle permanently traps the accumulated revenue

operational_questions: With USDC (6 decimals), what is the minimum supply that causes truncation to 0?
Does the trapped revenue accumulate indefinitely with each distribution cycle?
Is there any withdrawal mechanism for undistributed dust?
validation_requirements: Deploy with USDC as distributable token, mint 1000 ERC20 tokens, deposit 100 USDC, verify entitlement == 0
Verify no function exists to recover trapped funds
Calculate precision loss for DAI (18 decimals) vs USDC (6 decimals) to compare

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: No attacker needed - this is an inherent math bug
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Revenue accumulates in the contract from managed NFTs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Distribution executes but entitlementPerUnit = 0 for 6-decimal tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Revenue permanently locked in contract with no recovery path
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - automatic loss during normal operations

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All revenue from 6-decimal tokens (USDC, USDT) permanently unclaimable
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery mechanism exists; funds are permanently locked

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Growing ERC20 balances in contract that never decrease despite distributions
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Holders receiving 0 entitlement for tokens with actual balance

threats.title: Missing Break in _afterTokenTransfer Swap-and-Pop Causes Incomplete Holder Removal
threats.hypothesis_description: In _afterTokenTransfer, after performing swap-and-pop to remove `from` from holders, the loop does NOT break. It also does NOT decrement i. This means the element swapped from the end of the array into position i is never checked (i increments past it). For addresses appearing multiple times (due to the address(0) bug in OPS-H002), not all entries are removed. When the loop finds `from` at index i, it swaps with the last element and pops, but the swapped-in element at index i is skipped when i increments. Adjacent duplicate entries near the end of the array persist.
threats.investigation_rationale: This compounds the address(0) accumulation bug. Even when _afterTokenTransfer tries to clean up entries (e.g., during mints where from=address(0)), it fails to remove all duplicates due to this loop logic error.

proof_of_signal.pattern: swap-and-pop in loop without break or i-- after removal; swapped element at index i is skipped

preconditions: Duplicate entries exist in holders array (via burns creating address(0) duplicates or zero-value transfer attack)
A transfer/mint/burn causes _afterTokenTransfer to attempt removal of the duplicated address

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: After swap-and-pop at index i, the element previously at the end is now at i, but i++ means it's never inspected
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever duplicate entries exist and a removal is attempted

operational_questions: How many duplicates survive a single removal attempt?
Does this interact with OPS-H002 to make address(0) entries irremovable?
Can the holders array grow monotonically despite removal attempts?
validation_requirements: Create 5 address(0) entries via burns, then mint to trigger _afterTokenTransfer for address(0), count remaining entries
Verify that elements swapped to position i are indeed skipped

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create multiple duplicate entries for an address in holders (via OPS-H001 or OPS-H002)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Observe that not all entries are removed due to missing break/decrement
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Residual entries cause ongoing distribution gas waste or reward amplification

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Holders array cannot be fully cleaned up, causing persistent gas overhead in distributions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery mechanism; stale entries permanently increase distribution costs

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: holders.length remaining high despite removal attempts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Removal operations not reducing array size as expected

threats.title: Non-Standard ERC20 Tokens Without SafeERC20 Can Permanently Lock Distribution State
threats.hypothesis_description: The distribute() function uses `toDistribute.transfer(recipient, entitlement)` which expects a bool return value. Tokens like USDT that don't return a bool cause the Solidity 0.8.x ABI decoder to revert. Since distribute() is protected by nonReentrant and is called within the LockedForDistribution state, a revert from a non-standard token permanently locks the contract: LockedForDistribution stays true, blocking all transfers, mints, and burns. Additionally in _withdrawBalancesTo, the same pattern is used with `require(result, 'unsuccessful withdrawal')`, which also breaks for non-bool-returning tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The project documentation lists USDT as a target distributable token. USDT is well-known for not returning bool on transfer(). Solidity 0.8.12 ABI decoding will revert when the return data is empty or incorrect size.
deployment_assumptions: USDT or other non-standard ERC20 is included in distributableERC20s
threats.config_evidence: Documentation states 'External Integrations: Vetted ERC20 stablecoins (e.g., USDC, DAI, USDT)'. USDT is explicitly mentioned as a target token.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(distributableERC20s[j]).transfer(recipient, entitlement) — raw transfer without SafeERC20; USDT returns no bool, causing ABI decode revert

preconditions: A distributableERC20 does not return bool from transfer() (e.g., USDT)
Distribution is initiated with balance > 0 for that token
At least one approved holder exists

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No SafeERC20 used anywhere; raw IERC20.transfer() calls will revert for USDT
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin must include non-standard token in distributableERC20s (documentation suggests they will)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: First distribution after non-standard token has balance

operational_questions: Does USDT on the target chain (Althea-L1) return bool from transfer()?
Can the distribution be unstuck if it reverts?
Does the Althea-L1 EVM handle non-returning transfers differently?
validation_requirements: Test distribute() with actual USDT contract on target chain
Verify LockedForDistribution cannot be reset without completing distribution
Check if SafeERC20 would resolve the issue

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: No attacker needed - automatic failure when USDT is configured as distributable
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Revenue from managed NFTs includes USDT, deposited into the contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: distribute() is called; transfer() to first holder reverts due to missing return value
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: LockedForDistribution remains true; all contract operations permanently frozen
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - occurs during normal operations with USDT

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete protocol freeze - all transfers, mints, burns, and distributions permanently blocked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery mechanism; contract is permanently bricked
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All contract functions gated by LockedForDistribution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Distribution reverts repeatedly at same holder index

threats.title: ManagedNFTs Modification During Active Withdrawal Cycle Corrupts nextWithdrawal State
threats.hypothesis_description: If releaseManagedNFT is called while nextWithdrawal > 0 (partial withdrawal in progress), the swap-and-pop on ManagedNFTs modifies the array without adjusting nextWithdrawal. If the released NFT is at index < nextWithdrawal, the element swapped from the end (not yet withdrawn) takes that position and is skipped. If enough NFTs are released such that ManagedNFTs.length < nextWithdrawal, the equality check `nextWithdrawal == ManagedNFTs.length` always fails, and nextWithdrawal is never reset to 0. The withdrawal loop `for (i = nextWithdrawal; i < limit; i++)` never executes when nextWithdrawal > array length, creating a permanent stall.
threats.investigation_rationale: There is no guard on releaseManagedNFT or addManagedNFT checking whether a withdrawal cycle is in progress. The owner can inadvertently corrupt the withdrawal state machine.

proof_of_signal.pattern: releaseManagedNFT uses swap-and-pop on ManagedNFTs without checking or adjusting nextWithdrawal; no guard prevents modification during active withdrawal

preconditions: Owner calls withdrawFromManagedNFTs with a partial batch (nextWithdrawal > 0)
Owner then calls releaseManagedNFT before the withdrawal cycle completes

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: nextWithdrawal cursor not adjusted when ManagedNFTs array is modified by release/add
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner to call releaseManagedNFT during active withdrawal - possible during routine management

operational_questions: Can nextWithdrawal exceed ManagedNFTs.length permanently?
Is there any mechanism to reset nextWithdrawal manually?
Can addManagedNFT be used as a workaround to increase array length past nextWithdrawal?
validation_requirements: Start a partial withdrawal, release an NFT, verify nextWithdrawal state
Test scenario where ManagedNFTs.length < nextWithdrawal after release
Attempt to complete withdrawal cycle after corruption

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner starts partial withdrawal (withdrawFromManagedNFTs(2) out of 4 NFTs)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Owner releases NFT at index < nextWithdrawal
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Array shrinks; if ManagedNFTs.length < nextWithdrawal, withdrawal permanently stalls
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Revenue from remaining NFTs cannot be collected; distributions lack funds

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Withdrawal process permanently stalled; revenue collection from all managed NFTs blocked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: May be recoverable by adding enough NFTs to increase array length past nextWithdrawal, but skipped NFTs lose one cycle of revenue

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - accidental scenario during routine management

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: nextWithdrawal > ManagedNFTs.length; WithdrawalFinished event never emitted after WithdrawalStarted
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Withdrawal functions execute but process 0 NFTs
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: releaseManagedNFT called between partial withdrawal batches

threats.title: MinDistributionPeriod=0 Creates Permanent Mint/Burn Deadlock
threats.hypothesis_description: When MinDistributionPeriod is 0, _isPastMinDistributionPeriod() returns true immediately after a distribution ends in the same block: (block.number - LastDistribution) = 0 >= 0. This means _beforeMintOrBurn() always reverts with 'must distribute before minting or burning'. Calling distribute() completes immediately (no holders need paying or it was just done), setting LastDistribution = current block. The subsequent mint/burn check fails again in the same block. mintAndDistribute() also fails because distribute completes, then mint triggers the check which immediately passes again. This creates a permanent deadlock on minting and burning.
threats.investigation_rationale: The constructor does not validate that MinDistributionPeriod > 0. A deployment with MinDistributionPeriod=0 permanently freezes token supply after the first distribution.
deployment_assumptions: MinDistributionPeriod is set to 0 during deployment
threats.config_evidence: Deployment script uses MinDistributionPeriod=10, but no validation in constructor prevents 0. Constructor at LiquidInfrastructureERC20.sol accepts any uint256.

proof_of_signal.pattern: return (block.number - LastDistribution) >= MinDistributionPeriod; — when MinDistributionPeriod=0, always true when holders exist and supply > 0

preconditions: MinDistributionPeriod is 0 at deployment
At least one holder exists with non-zero balance (totalSupply > 0)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No validation that MinDistributionPeriod > 0 in constructor; >= 0 is always true
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent after first distribution completes when holders and supply exist

operational_questions: Does mintAndDistribute() solve this by distributing then minting in the same call?
Can the owner change MinDistributionPeriod after deployment?
Is there any path to mint when the check always fails?
validation_requirements: Deploy with MinDistributionPeriod=0, mint tokens, distribute, attempt mint again
Verify mintAndDistribute() also fails
Confirm no setter exists for MinDistributionPeriod

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Owner mints tokens and a distribution occurs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All subsequent mints and burns fail permanently
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Token supply frozen; no new tokens can be issued or burned

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Token supply permanently frozen; no new participants can be onboarded via minting
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No setter for MinDistributionPeriod; contract must be redeployed
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: mint, burn, burnFrom, mintAndDistribute, burnAndDistribute, burnFromAndDistribute

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: All mint/burn transactions reverting after first distribution

threats.title: setDistributableERC20s Callable During Active Distribution Causes Permanent Lock
threats.hypothesis_description: setDistributableERC20s has no check for LockedForDistribution. If the owner calls it during an active distribution, the distributableERC20s array changes while erc20EntitlementPerUnit was sized for the old array. If the new array is shorter, accessing distributableERC20s[j] and erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j] in distribute() will have mismatched lengths. If the new array is longer, erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j] will revert with index out of bounds. Either way, the distribution reverts permanently, and LockedForDistribution stays true, freezing all contract operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code defect: setDistributableERC20s lacks a LockedForDistribution guard. While it requires owner action, the code should prevent this state corruption.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setDistributableERC20s has onlyOwner but no require(!LockedForDistribution); distribute iterates over both distributableERC20s and erc20EntitlementPerUnit assuming they match

preconditions: Owner calls setDistributableERC20s while LockedForDistribution is true
Distribution is partially complete (some holders already paid)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No LockedForDistribution check in setDistributableERC20s; array length mismatch with erc20EntitlementPerUnit causes OOB revert
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner to call during active distribution (mistake or malicious)

operational_questions: Does the OOB access cause a permanent revert in distribute()?
Can the owner fix the state by calling setDistributableERC20s back to the original array?
Is there any way to unlock the contract without completing distribution?
validation_requirements: Start distribution, call setDistributableERC20s mid-distribution, attempt to continue distribute()
Verify whether resetting the array to original values allows distribution to complete

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Owner calls setDistributableERC20s with different array length
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Next distribute() call reverts due to array length mismatch
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Contract permanently locked; no transfers, mints, or burns possible

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete permanent freeze of all contract operations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner may recover by resetting distributableERC20s to exact original values (needs investigation)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: setDistributableERC20s call between DistributionStarted and DistributionFinished events
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: distribute() reverting repeatedly after setDistributableERC20s call
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Admin function call during locked state

threats.title: releaseManagedNFT require(true) No-Op Validation Allows Silent NFT Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: releaseManagedNFT contains `require(true, 'unable to find released NFT in ManagedNFTs')` which is a no-op — it always passes regardless of whether the NFT was actually found and removed from the array. If the owner passes an nftContract address that is NOT in ManagedNFTs (e.g., typo, already released, or never added via addManagedNFT), the NFT is transferred out via transferFrom but the ManagedNFTs array is not modified. This leaves stale entries pointing to NFTs no longer owned by this contract, causing withdrawFromManagedNFTs to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear code defect — the developer intended to validate that the NFT was found and removed, but the require condition is hardcoded to true. The comment 'By this point the NFT should have been found' confirms the intended validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(true, 'unable to find released NFT in ManagedNFTs') — condition is literal `true`, never fails

preconditions: Owner calls releaseManagedNFT with an address not in ManagedNFTs array
This contract owns the NFT at the given address (transferFrom succeeds)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: require(true) is a no-op; NFT transferred out without array modification if not found
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner error or intentional misuse

operational_questions: What happens when withdrawFromManagedNFTs encounters a stale ManagedNFTs entry?
Does the stale entry cause a revert or silent failure?
Can the owner recover by re-adding and releasing the correct NFT?
validation_requirements: Call releaseManagedNFT with address not in ManagedNFTs, verify require(true) passes
Add NFT via addManagedNFT, release with wrong address, verify stale entry remains
Attempt withdrawFromManagedNFTs with stale entry

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner calls releaseManagedNFT with incorrect or non-existent address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: require(true) passes silently; ManagedNFTs array unchanged
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Stale entry in ManagedNFTs points to unowned NFT
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: withdrawFromManagedNFTs reverts when reaching the stale entry, blocking all subsequent withdrawals

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Withdrawal cycle blocked by stale ManagedNFTs entry; revenue collection halted
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner may need to add the stale NFT back and release it properly, or the withdrawal must skip past it via partial calls

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ManagedNFTs containing addresses not owned by this contract
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: releaseManagedNFT called without corresponding ManagedNFTs entry change

threats.title: Disapproved Holders Dilute All Distributions for Approved Holders
threats.hypothesis_description: The entitlement calculation uses totalSupply() as the denominator: `entitlement = balance / totalSupply()`. However, disapproved holders' tokens still count in totalSupply but they are skipped during distribution (isApprovedHolder check). This means disapproved holders' proportional share is silently withheld — approved holders receive less than their fair share relative to the approved-holder supply. The withheld portion stays in the contract with no redistribution mechanism. A disapproved holder also cannot transfer their tokens (allowlist check fails) and can only burn (if distribution is current).
threats.investigation_rationale: The owner can disapprove holders at any time. Each disapproved holder with balance dilutes all approved holders' distributions permanently. This is a logic error in the entitlement calculation — the denominator should be the sum of approved holders' balances.

proof_of_signal.pattern: entitlement = balance / this.totalSupply() — totalSupply includes disapproved holders' balances; distribute skips disapproved via isApprovedHolder check

preconditions: At least one holder is disapproved while still holding tokens
The disapproved holder's balance is a significant portion of totalSupply

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Entitlement denominator is totalSupply (includes disapproved); distribution skips disapproved; mismatch causes underpayment
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner must disapprove a holder who retains tokens
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every distribution cycle while disapproved holders hold tokens

operational_questions: Can a disapproved holder burn their tokens to reduce the dilution?
Is there a mechanism for the owner to force-burn a disapproved holder's tokens?
How much dilution occurs with realistic disapproved holder balances?
validation_requirements: Disapprove a holder with 50% of supply, verify approved holders receive only ~50% of expected distributions
Verify the undistributed portion remains permanently in the contract

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner disapproves a holder (for KYC failure or other reason)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Disapproved holder cannot transfer tokens (allowlist blocks it)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Every distribution calculates entitlement using full totalSupply including disapproved balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Approved holders receive diluted payments; the remainder is trapped

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Systematic underpayment to all approved holders proportional to disapproved holder balance
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Disapproved holder could burn their tokens (if distribution is current), but they have no incentive to do so

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Disapproved holders with non-zero balances; distribution payouts less than expected
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: disapproveHolder calls followed by distributions with residual balances

threats.title: Allowlist Modification During Active Distribution Creates Inconsistent Payouts
threats.hypothesis_description: approveHolder and disapproveHolder have no LockedForDistribution guard. During a multi-batch distribution (LockedForDistribution=true, partial distribute calls), the owner can approve or disapprove holders between batches. Holders already distributed to were checked against the old allowlist state; remaining holders are checked against the new state. This means a holder at index > nextDistributionRecipient can be disapproved mid-distribution, losing their entitlement for this cycle despite being approved when entitlements were calculated. Similarly, a newly approved holder would receive nothing since they're not in the holders array yet.
threats.investigation_rationale: The LockedForDistribution lock protects transfers but not admin allowlist operations. This creates an inconsistency window within a single distribution cycle.

proof_of_signal.pattern: approveHolder/disapproveHolder have onlyOwner but no require(!LockedForDistribution); distribute checks isApprovedHolder per-holder during iteration

preconditions: Distribution is in progress (LockedForDistribution = true)
Distribution is being executed in multiple batches (nextDistributionRecipient < holders.length)
Owner calls approveHolder or disapproveHolder between batch calls

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No LockedForDistribution check on allowlist functions; allowlist changes take effect mid-distribution
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner to modify allowlist during active distribution
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between partial distribute() calls during a locked distribution

operational_questions: Can the owner selectively exclude holders from specific distributions?
Does the skipped holder's entitlement get redistributed or trapped?
Is this exploitable for selective yield theft?
validation_requirements: Start multi-batch distribution, disapprove a later holder between batches, verify they receive nothing
Verify the disapproved holder's entitlement remains in the contract

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Distribution begins in batches (distribute(5) out of 20 holders)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Owner calls disapproveHolder on holder at index 15
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: When distribution reaches index 15, isApprovedHolder returns false, share is skipped
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Holder 15's entitlement remains in contract, effectively redistributed to next cycle or trapped as dust

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Selective denial of entitled distributions to specific holders
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Lost entitlement cannot be retroactively claimed; may be partially recovered in next distribution
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: distribute — specific holder payouts within a single cycle

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: disapproveHolder call between DistributionStarted and DistributionFinished events
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Specific holders receiving 0 distribution despite holding tokens
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Admin calls interleaved with distribution batches
